standards.statement: The practice has a patient rights policy.
standards.intent_tooltip: The leaders of a practice are primarily responsible for the way in which that practice treats its patients. The leaders need to know and understand patient and family rights and their practice's responsibilities as specified in laws, charters and regulations. The leaders then provide direction to ensure that personnel throughout the practice assume responsibility for protecting these rights. To protect and advance patient rights effectively, the leaders work collaboratively and seek to understand their responsibilities in relation to the community served by the practice. Patient and family rights are a fundamental element of all contact between the practice personnel and patients and families. Policies and procedures are developed and implemented to ensure that all personnel are aware of and respond to patient and family rights issues including their role in supporting patients' and families' rights to participate in the care process and the right to the provision of all information requested by patients and families to enable them to do so. The patient's rights policy is appropriate to the patient's age, understanding and language. When written communication is not effective or appropriate, the patient and family are informed of their rights in a manner they can understand.

criteria.description: Patient and family rights are identified in a policy and documented in accordance with relevant and current laws, charters and regulations.
criteria.guideline: Patient and family rights are internationally accepted and implemented. Country specific patient rights charters must be referenced in the policy. The practice policy must include at least the right to: • A healthy and safe environment • Participation in decision-making • Access to health care • Ethical health care • Choice of health services • Confidentiality and privacy • Culturally sensitive care • Informed consent • Refusal of treatment • A second opinion • Complaints about health services

criteria.description: Documented evidence of personnel training in relation to these policies and procedures is available.
criteria.guideline: Evidence of such training can exist in various forms such as formal in-service training sessions, meetings where policies and procedures are discussed, staff acknowledging in writing that they have studied policies and procedures, etc.

criteria.description: There is a patient rights charter which is prominently displayed and patients are made aware of their rights.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured on observation and by documented evidence that patients are made aware of their rights. This can be achieved by drawing their attention to the poster or by having copies of the charter available. Intent of 3.1.2 Patients or those making decisions on their behalf (i.e. in the case of minors or patients who lack mental capacity due to physical or mental illness) may decide not to proceed with the planned care or treatment or not to continue care or treatment after it has been initiated. The practice informs patients and families about their right to make these decisions, about the potential outcomes that could result from these decisions and about their responsibilities related to such decisions. Patients and families are given information on any care and treatment alternatives. Personnel are informed of their responsibility to implement and respect the choices of patients. 3.1.3. The practice takes measures to protect patient privacy. Intent of 3.1.3 The practice ensures that the patient's need for privacy is respected, especially when the patient is providing personal information and undergoing clinical examination. Patients may desire privacy from other personnel, other patients and even from family members. Medical and other health information, when documented and collected in a patient record or other form, is important for understanding the patient, his or her needs and for providing care over time. The practice respects such information as confidential and has implemented policies and procedures that protect such information from loss or misuse. The personnel respect the confidentiality of patient information by not leaving patient files, results, etc. where they might be visible to members of the public and by not holding patient-related discussions where they may be overheard by other patients or visitors. Such carelessness with patient information can result in loss of dignity or employment for the patient and may result in damage to personal or family relationships. These consequences can follow carelessness by the personnel of the practice, or by family members or others not authorised to have access to the information who have obtained information due to the carelessness of personnel.

standards.statement: The practice respects the rights of patients and their families to refuse or discontinue treatment. 3.1.1.1. Patient and family rights are identified in a policy and documented in accordance with relevant and current laws, charters and regulations. Guideline statement: Patient and family rights are internationally accepted and implemented. Country specific patient rights charters must be referenced in the policy. The practice policy must include at least the right to: • A healthy and safe environment • Participation in decision-making • Access to health care • Ethical health care • Choice of health services • Confidentiality and privacy • Culturally sensitive care • Informed consent • Refusal of treatment • A second opinion • Complaints about health services 3.1.1.2. Documented evidence of personnel training in relation to these policies and procedures is available. Guideline statement: Evidence of such training can exist in various forms such as formal in-service training sessions, meetings where policies and procedures are discussed, staff acknowledging in writing that they have studied policies and procedures, etc. 3.1.1.3. There is a patient rights charter which is prominently displayed and patients are made aware of their rights. Guideline statement: Compliance will be measured on observation and by documented evidence that patients are made aware of their rights. This can be achieved by drawing their attention to the poster or by having copies of the charter available.
standards.intent_tooltip: Patients or those making decisions on their behalf (i.e. in the case of minors or patients who lack mental capacity due to physical or mental illness) may decide not to proceed with the planned care or treatment or not to continue care or treatment after it has been initiated. The practice informs patients and families about their right to make these decisions, about the potential outcomes that could result from these decisions and about their responsibilities related to such decisions. Patients and families are given information on any care and treatment alternatives. Personnel are informed of their responsibility to implement and respect the choices of patients.

criteria.description: Patients are informed about their condition and the proposed treatment.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be assessed during the patient record audit and/or patient interviews.

criteria.description: The practice informs patients and their families about their rights to refuse or discontinue treatment, and the consequences of such decisions.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be assessed during the patient record audit and/or patient interviews.

criteria.description: When patients/carers refuse or discontinue treatment, the consultation regarding their decision is accurately and contemporaneously recorded and includes the details of the discussion regarding the consequences of the decision.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be assessed during the patient record audit.

standards.statement: The practice takes measures to protect patient privacy.
standards.intent_tooltip: The practice ensures that the patient's need for privacy is respected, especially when the patient is providing personal information and undergoing clinical examination. Patients may desire privacy from other personnel, other patients and even from family members. Medical and other health information, when documented and collected in a patient record or other form, is important for understanding the patient, his or her needs and for providing care over time. The practice respects such information as confidential and has implemented policies and procedures that protect such information from loss or misuse. The personnel respect the confidentiality of patient information by not leaving patient files, results, etc. where they might be visible to members of the public and by not holding patient-related discussions where they may be overheard by other patients or visitors. Such carelessness with patient information can result in loss of dignity or employment for the patient and may result in damage to personal or family relationships. These consequences can follow carelessness by the personnel of the practice, or by family members or others not authorised to have access to the information who have obtained information due to the carelessness of personnel.

criteria.description: The patient’s need for privacy is protected during all examinations, procedures and treatments.
criteria.guideline: This must be evident in all patient care areas.

criteria.description: Patient privacy is protected when providing personal information. 3.1.4. The practice has a clearly defined process for obtaining consent. Intent of 3.1.4 One of the main ways that patients are involved in their care decisions is by granting informed consent. The patient must be provided with all information relating to planned care to enable him or her to make decisions. The consent process is clearly defined by the practice in policies and procedures. Relevant laws and regulations are incorporated into the policies and procedures. Informed consent for care sometimes requires that people other than or in addition to the patient be involved in decisions about the patient's care. This is especially true when the patient does not have the mental or physical capacity to make care decisions, when culture or custom designate that others make care decisions or when the patient is a child. When the patient cannot make decisions regarding his or her care a surrogate decision-maker is identified. When someone other than the patient gives consent, that individual is noted in the patient's record.
criteria.guideline: This must be evident in all patient care areas including administrative and reception areas. Patient privacy should be safeguarded at all times, including when providing personal information to nonclinical personnel. It is not always necessary to provide privacy by means of physical barriers such as walls and doors. Distance between waiting patients and those speaking with practice personnel can also provide the required privacy when less sensitive information is being exchanged. For example, a line can be drawn on the floor some distance away from the registration desk or pharmacy counter. Patients can be requested to wait behind the line and approach the counter only when providing their details or receiving their medication. Background noise, e.g. music or patient education videos, can help to prevent conversations with patients being overheard.

criteria.description: The patient’s right to privacy is protected for all forms of counselling.
criteria.guideline: This must be evident in all patient care areas.

criteria.description: Policies and procedures to prevent the loss or misuse of patient information are implemented.
criteria.guideline: This criterion measures more than just the information contained in the patient record. The confidentiality agreement that is signed by personnel must include the protection of all patient related information including for example, discussing patient details with anyone not involved in the patient’s care, comments on social media etc. Compliance will be measured by evidence of a signed confidentiality agreement and by examining the incident management system in the practice to establish if any complaints or incidents have been reported involving a breach of patient confidentiality.

criteria.description: When appropriate, patients are permitted to be accompanied by a family member/care-giver during consultations.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during patient interviews and observation.

standards.statement: The practice has a clearly defined process for obtaining consent.
standards.intent_tooltip: One of the main ways that patients are involved in their care decisions is by granting informed consent. The patient must be provided with all information relating to planned care to enable him or her to make decisions. The consent process is clearly defined by the practice in policies and procedures. Relevant laws and regulations are incorporated into the policies and procedures. Informed consent for care sometimes requires that people other than or in addition to the patient be involved in decisions about the patient's care. This is especially true when the patient does not have the mental or physical capacity to make care decisions, when culture or custom designate that others make care decisions or when the patient is a child. When the patient cannot make decisions regarding his or her care a surrogate decision-maker is identified. When someone other than the patient gives consent, that individual is noted in the patient's record.

criteria.description: The practice has a documented policy outlining the procedure for obtaining general consent for treatment.
criteria.guideline: This can be achieved for example by obtaining general consent for treatment from patients when they register at the practice. Compliance will be assessed during the patient record audit.

criteria.description: The practice has a documented policy outlining the procedure for obtaining consent in the case of patients who are unable to grant consent for themselves by way of age or mental/physical incapacity, which is in accordance with the relevant laws and regulations.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be assessed during the patient record audit.

criteria.description: Documented consent is obtained from patients for health information to be provided to a third party.
criteria.guideline: Evidence of compliance with this criterion may be found in different documents. For example, patients may give consent for information to be shared with health funders as part of their membership registration. Consent is required whenever health information is shared for example with employers, family members or carers, lawyers and insurance companies. 3.1.5. The practice informs patients and their families about the processes which it has instituted to receive and act on complaints, conflicts and differences of opinion about patient care and the patient’s right to participate in those processes. Intent of 3.1.5 Patients have a right to voice complaints about their care and to have those complaints reviewed and where possible resolved. Decisions regarding care sometimes present questions, conflicts or other dilemmas for the practice and the patient, family or other decision-makers. The practice has established processes for seeking resolution to such dilemmas and complaints. The practice identifies in policies and procedures those who need to be involved in the processes and how the patient and family participate. 3.2.1. Patients are informed of the processes to access the practice services. Intent of 3.2.1 Patients and services working in collaboration with the practice need to know how and when to contact the practice to access care. It is reasonable to expect most practices to offer care during normal office hours.

criteria.description: Where practice members use patient information for research, approval has been obtained from the relevant ethics committee and from patients themselves where required.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence will be required.

standards.statement: The practice informs patients and their families about the processes which it has instituted to receive and act on complaints, conflicts and differences of opinion about patient care and the patient’s right to participate in those processes.
standards.intent_tooltip: Patients have a right to voice complaints about their care and to have those complaints reviewed and where possible resolved. Decisions regarding care sometimes present questions, conflicts or other dilemmas for the practice and the patient, family or other decision-makers. The practice has established processes for seeking resolution to such dilemmas and complaints. The practice identifies in policies and procedures those who need to be involved in the processes and how the patient and family participate.

criteria.description: There is a documented policy outlining the mechanism to allow for the hearing of complaints and how to act upon them which is implemented.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence of implementation of the complaints procedure, e.g. complaints log and the documentation relating to the investigation and resolution of complaints, must be made available for assessment.

criteria.description: Patients are aware of their right to voice complaints and the processes by which to do so, internally as well as externally where applicable.
criteria.guideline: The complaints process must be prominently displayed in the practice. Results obtained from patient interviews and observation will determine the level of compliance.

criteria.description: A nominated individual within the practice is responsible for managing the complaints and ensuring that the complaints policy is implemented.
criteria.guideline: This should be a senior person in the practice and is usually the solo practitioner or the practice manager.

criteria.description: A nominated individual within the practice is responsible to oversee the investigation of and response to the complaint.
criteria.guideline: Complainants must be kept informed of the progress of the investigation into the complaint. If there is a legitimate delay, they should be informed of the reason for the delay and provided with regular updates as to the progress of the management of their complaint. Documented evidence is required.

criteria.description: Complaints are monitored and repetitions or patterns are identified.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence of such monitoring must be available.

criteria.description: Any opportunities for improvement identified from the investigation of complaints are implemented.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence is required. For efficiency of service delivery, appointment systems are recommended as best practice. 3.2.2. The practice has an appointment system and a process for recognising cases requiring more urgent attention and providing patients with longer appointments when appropriate. Intent of 3.2.2 For efficiency of service delivery, appointment systems are recommended as best practice. The system must be flexible in determining the order in which patients are seen to accommodate patients' needs for urgent care, non-urgent care, complex care, planned chronic disease management, preventive healthcare and longer consultations. Members of the practice team should be sensitive to the need for longer appointment slots when the need for a longer investigation could be anticipated (for example, when the patient is attending for multiple or complex problems, or procedures). If the practice is involved in training or research which makes it necessary for third parties to be present during the consultation, the patient is informed of this and given the opportunity to offer or withhold consent.

standards.statement: Patients are informed of the processes to access the practice services.
standards.intent_tooltip: Patients and services working in collaboration with the practice need to know how and when to contact the practice to access care. It is reasonable to expect most practices to offer care during normal office hours. 3.1.4.4. Where practice members use patient information for research, approval has been obtained from the relevant ethics committee and from patients themselves where required. Guideline statement: Documented evidence will be required. 3.1.5.1. There is a documented policy outlining the mechanism to allow for the hearing of complaints and how to act upon them which is implemented. Guideline statement: Documented evidence of implementation of the complaints procedure, e.g. complaints log and the documentation relating to the investigation and resolution of complaints, must be made available for assessment. 3.1.5.2. Patients are aware of their right to voice complaints and the processes by which to do so, internally as well as externally where applicable. Guideline statement: The complaints process must be prominently displayed in the practice. Results obtained from patient interviews and observation will determine the level of compliance. 3.1.5.3. A nominated individual within the practice is responsible for managing the complaints and ensuring that the complaints policy is implemented. Guideline statement: This should be a senior person in the practice and is usually the solo practitioner or the practice manager. 3.1.5.4. A nominated individual within the practice is responsible to oversee the investigation of and response to the complaint. Guideline statement: Complainants must be kept informed of the progress of the investigation into the complaint. If there is a legitimate delay, they should be informed of the reason for the delay and provided with regular updates as to the progress of the management of their complaint. Documented evidence is required. 3.1.5.5. Complaints are monitored and repetitions or patterns are identified. Guideline statement: Documented evidence of such monitoring must be available. 3.1.5.6. Any opportunities for improvement identified from the investigation of complaints are implemented. Guideline statement: Documented evidence is required. For efficiency of service delivery, appointment systems are recommended as best practice.

criteria.description: When patients register with the practice they are informed of the range of services offered by the practice, opening hours, contact details and after hours’ care arrangements, where applicable.
criteria.guideline: It is advisable to provide this information in printed form as patients may not remember what has been told to them. As a minimum, patients must acknowledge receipt of the information by signing (on the registration form) that they have been informed.

criteria.description: Where the practice has an 'on hold' telephone message, it includes a message for an alternative number to be used in an emergency.
criteria.guideline: This criterion is only applicable to practices where the telephone system diverts to an ‘on hold’ message when the line is busy during business hours. The purpose of this requirement is to assist patients who are dealing with an emergency during peak business hours when it might be difficult to get through to the practice because of the high volume of calls. If the practice does not have an ‘on hold’ system this criterion will be not applicable.

criteria.description: A message on the practice’s telephone answering machine, call diversion system or paging system provide information to patients on how to obtain care outside the practice’s normal opening hours.
criteria.guideline: The intention of this criterion is to ensure that patients are assisted with the relevant information outside of business hours.

criteria.description: The practice renders services based on the needs of the population, during the hours that they publish.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence can be provided by time cards, duty rosters etc. and can also be verified during patient and personnel interviews.

standards.statement: The practice has an appointment system and a process for recognising cases requiring more urgent attention and providing patients with longer appointments when appropriate.
standards.intent_tooltip: For efficiency of service delivery, appointment systems are recommended as best practice. The system must be flexible in determining the order in which patients are seen to accommodate patients' needs for urgent care, non-urgent care, complex care, planned chronic disease management, preventive healthcare and longer consultations. Members of the practice team should be sensitive to the need for longer appointment slots when the need for a longer investigation could be anticipated (for example, when the patient is attending for multiple or complex problems, or procedures). If the practice is involved in training or research which makes it necessary for third parties to be present during the consultation, the patient is informed of this and given the opportunity to offer or withhold consent.

criteria.description: There is a flexible appointment system for consultations.
criteria.guideline: This criterion will be scored compliant in practices that do not utilise an appointment system if it can be demonstrated that patients who request an appointment at a specific time (for example, because of business commitments, travel arrangements etc.) are accommodated.

criteria.description: There is a documented system for fast-tracking the very ill, the elderly and frail, and pregnant women.
criteria.guideline: Both clinical and administrative personnel must be able to describe the practice’s system for fast-tracking patients and the process for seeking urgent medical assistance from a clinical staff member. 3.2.3. At registration, sufficient details are taken from the patient to ensure that the patient can be contacted by the practice when necessary and that the clinical team has sufficient background information to provide adequate care to patients. Intent of 3.2.3 Accurate contact details are essential in order to follow up results, recall patients for chronic disease monitoring and contact patients or nominated next of kin in emergency situations that may arise. Background medical information is essential to the provision of adequate care, but can be adequately obtained by administrative personnel in the first instance with further detail elicited during the consultation if necessary. The following details should be obtained at registration of a new patient as a minimum: a) current address b) telephone numbers c) next of kin d) who to contact in an emergency e) previous medical history f) previous surgical history g) current medication (prescribed and over the counter medications) h) allergies i) immunisations j) health risk factors e.g. smoking, alcohol consumption, physical activity k) patient signature

criteria.description: Patients who are waiting are advised of any delays that may be experienced in receiving attention.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured during patient and personnel interviews.

criteria.description: There is a system to ensure that patients are seen within the shortest possible time.
criteria.guideline: To be compliant with this criterion personnel must be able to describe the patient journey from arriving at the practice until they leave. Administrative processes must be simple, standardised and streamlined as far as possible.

criteria.description: Patients are offered longer appointments when clinically indicated.
criteria.guideline: The practice must identify those instances where experience has shown that longer appointments will be required. This is important in the event where patients require preparation for their consultation. This criterion links to 2.2.2.4 above where patients will not be seen in the shortest possible time if the appointments run over time with preceding patients.

criteria.description: The patient’s consent is obtained if third parties are expected to be present during the consultation.
criteria.guideline: This can apply to students, researchers, other practice personnel and even to the patient’s family and care- givers. Documented evidence must be provided.

standards.statement: At registration, sufficient details are taken from the patient to ensure that the patient can be contacted by the practice when necessary and that the clinical team has sufficient background information to provide adequate care to patients.
standards.intent_tooltip: Accurate contact details are essential in order to follow up results, recall patients for chronic disease monitoring and contact patients or nominated next of kin in emergency situations that may arise. Background medical information is essential to the provision of adequate care, but can be adequately obtained by administrative personnel in the first instance with further detail elicited during the consultation if necessary. The following details should be obtained at registration of a new patient as a minimum: a) current address b) telephone numbers c) next of kin d) who to contact in an emergency e) previous medical history f) previous surgical history g) current medication (prescribed and over the counter medications) h) allergies i) immunisations j) health risk factors e.g. smoking, alcohol consumption, physical activity k) patient signature

criteria.description: New patients to the practice are asked to complete a form detailing at least a) – k) in the standard intent above.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.description: Patients are asked to update the practice if their contact details change.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit. 3.2.4. Patients are able to obtain advice or information related to their clinical care by telephone and electronic means. Intent of 3.2.4 Where patients who are known to the practice request information or follow up regarding a condition for which they have previously consulted and the practitioner determines that it is clinically safe not to review the patient in a face to face consultation, the patient has access to the relevant information by telephone or electronic communication. The practice has a documented policy regarding such communication that clearly outlines: a) Limitations of use b) Positive identification of the patient c) Timeframe for a response from a practitioner. d) That the patient was made aware of any costs involved e) Documentation of the communication in the patient record 3.2.5. Patient and family education promotes the concept of taking responsibility for one’s own health care. Intent of 3.2.5 Every patient is offered the information and education he or she requires. All clinical personnel within the practice work collaboratively to provide education in a co-ordinated manner. Personnel collaboration helps to ensure that the information patients and families receive is comprehensive, consistent and as effective as possible. Education is focused on the specific knowledge and skills the patient and his or her family will need to make care decisions, participate in care and continue care at home. Variables such as educational literacy, beliefs and limitations are taken into account. Each practice decides on the placement and format of educational assessment, planning and delivery of information. Education regarding high risk

criteria.description: Health funder details are accurately recorded for each new patient when required.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

standards.statement: Patients are able to obtain advice or information related to their clinical care by telephone and electronic means.
standards.intent_tooltip: Where patients who are known to the practice request information or follow up regarding a condition for which they have previously consulted and the practitioner determines that it is clinically safe not to review the patient in a face to face consultation, the patient has access to the relevant information by telephone or electronic communication. The practice has a documented policy regarding such communication that clearly outlines: a) Limitations of use b) Positive identification of the patient c) Timeframe for a response from a practitioner. d) That the patient was made aware of any costs involved e) Documentation of the communication in the patient record

criteria.description: The practice implements a policy on telephonic and electronic communication with patients that details at least a) – e) in the standard intent above.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during personnel and patient interviews, patient record audits and by observation.

criteria.description: There is evidence of practice/patient telephone or electronic advice and information in the patient health records.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.description: The practice can demonstrate how it receives and returns telephone and electronic messages from patients.
criteria.guideline: Personnel must be able to describe the process of which telephone calls are immediately put through to the practitioner and what process is followed to ensure that calls are returned timeously when they are not. Electronic messages can take the form of e-mails, text messages or messages received on messaging applications. health issues relevant to the local population is routinely provided by the practice. Standardised materials and processes are used where possible. Information provided by the practice may include when to resume daily activities, preventive practices relevant to the patient's condition and, when appropriate, information on coping with disease or disability. 3.3.1. A system for the storage, retrieval, retention and destruction of health records that meets the needs of confidentiality and safety is implemented. Intent of 3.3.1 Policies and procedures as well as managerial supervision ensure the safety and confidentiality of patient records. The practice develops and implements a policy that guides the storage, retrieval, retention and destruction of patient records and other data and information. Patient records and other data and information are retained for sufficient periods to comply with law and regulation and support patient care, the management of the practice, legal documentation, research and education. The retention

standards.statement: Patient and family education promotes the concept of taking responsibility for one’s own health care.
standards.intent_tooltip: Every patient is offered the information and education he or she requires. All clinical personnel within the practice work collaboratively to provide education in a co-ordinated manner. Personnel collaboration helps to ensure that the information patients and families receive is comprehensive, consistent and as effective as possible. Education is focused on the specific knowledge and skills the patient and his or her family will need to make care decisions, participate in care and continue care at home. Variables such as educational literacy, beliefs and limitations are taken into account. Each practice decides on the placement and format of educational assessment, planning and delivery of information. Education regarding high risk 3.2.3.3. Health funder details are accurately recorded for each new patient when required. Guideline statement: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit. 3.2.4.1. The practice implements a policy on telephonic and electronic communication with patients that details at least a) – e) in the standard intent above. Guideline statement: Compliance will be verified during personnel and patient interviews, patient record audits and by observation. 3.2.4.2. There is evidence of practice/patient telephone or electronic advice and information in the patient health records. Guideline statement: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit. 3.2.4.3. The practice can demonstrate how it receives and returns telephone and electronic messages from patients. Guideline statement: Personnel must be able to describe the process of which telephone calls are immediately put through to the practitioner and what process is followed to ensure that calls are returned timeously when they are not. Electronic messages can take the form of e-mails, text messages or messages received on messaging applications. health issues relevant to the local population is routinely provided by the practice. Standardised materials and processes are used where possible. Information provided by the practice may include when to resume daily activities, preventive practices relevant to the patient's condition and, when appropriate, information on coping with disease or disability.

criteria.description: The practice offers relevant health education to its patients in a planned and consistent manner which enables patients to meet their on-going health needs and achieve their health goals.
criteria.guideline: This will be assessed based on the evaluation of the patient education activities in relation to the services offered as well as the individual patient education during consultation for example, lifestyle advice, when to seek medical attention - how urgently and at what level of care, etc. Compliance will be verified during patient interviews and patient record audits.

criteria.description: Posters and pamphlets regarding high risk and commonly treated conditions relevant to the practice population (e.g. HIV, TB, Diabetes, Hypertension) are visibly displayed and accessible in the waiting room and routinely provided to patients during the consultation when appropriate.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during patient interviews, patient record audits and by observation.

criteria.description: Patient and family education provided is noted in the patient record.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audits.

criteria.description: The practice identifies community resources which support continuing health promotion and disease prevention education and has contact details of these resources available.
criteria.guideline: The practice must identify those resources that are specific to its patient population for example, breastfeeding support, mental health support, diabetes society etc. Documented evidence is required.

criteria.description: There is documented evidence that patients are referred to these resources, where appropriate.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audits.

criteria.description: Information is provided in a manner and language that is understood by those making the care decisions.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during patient interviews.

criteria.description: Prior to a treatment being rendered, except in cases of emergency, the practice must ensure that risks regarding the relevant procedure have been advised to the patient or substitute decision maker where necessary.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audits. policy is consistent with the confidentiality and security of such information. When the retention period is complete, patient records and other data and information are destroyed appropriately. The policy will define: a) Levels of access for individual staff members b) The user's obligation to keep information confidential c) The process followed when confidentiality and/or security are violated d) Secure storage and retrieval of paper based and electronic patient records e) The protection of records against damage, fire, flood, theft, loss and electronic failure f) The arrangements for succession of patient records in the event of closure of the practice or death of the practitioner g) The criteria for selection and method of destruction of patient records Personnel members responsible for health record management must have suitable training and experience. Patient records must be readily available each time the patient visits the practice and therefore must be filed in such a way that they are easily identified. 3.3.2. Patient records contain the required information for the relevant condition. Intent of 3.3.2 The clinical record of each patient, whether handwritten or electronic, clearly documents all aspects of the patient/client management, including the results of the initial examination/assessment and evaluation, diagnosis, prognosis/plan of care, intervention/treatment, response to interventions/treatment, changes in patient/client status relative to the interventions/treatment, re-

standards.statement: A system for the storage, retrieval, retention and destruction of health records that meets the needs of confidentiality and safety is implemented.
standards.intent_tooltip: Policies and procedures as well as managerial supervision ensure the safety and confidentiality of patient records. The practice develops and implements a policy that guides the storage, retrieval, retention and destruction of patient records and other data and information. Patient records and other data and information are retained for sufficient periods to comply with law and regulation and support patient care, the management of the practice, legal documentation, research and education. The retention 3.2.5.1. The practice offers relevant health education to its patients in a planned and consistent manner which enables patients to meet their on-going health needs and achieve their health goals. Guideline statement: This will be assessed based on the evaluation of the patient education activities in relation to the services offered as well as the individual patient education during consultation for example, lifestyle advice, when to seek medical attention - how urgently and at what level of care, etc. Compliance will be verified during patient interviews and patient record audits. 3.2.5.2. Posters and pamphlets regarding high risk and commonly treated conditions relevant to the practice population (e.g. HIV, TB, Diabetes, Hypertension) are visibly displayed and accessible in the waiting room and routinely provided to patients during the consultation when appropriate. Guideline statement: Compliance will be verified during patient interviews, patient record audits and by observation. 3.2.5.3. Patient and family education provided is noted in the patient record. Guideline statement: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audits. 3.2.5.4. The practice identifies community resources which support continuing health promotion and disease prevention education and has contact details of these resources available. Guideline statement: The practice must identify those resources that are specific to its patient population for example, breastfeeding support, mental health support, diabetes society etc. Documented evidence is required. 3.2.5.5. There is documented evidence that patients are referred to these resources, where appropriate. Guideline statement: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audits. 3.2.5.6. Information is provided in a manner and language that is understood by those making the care decisions. Guideline statement: Compliance will be verified during patient interviews. 3.2.5.7. Prior to a treatment being rendered, except in cases of emergency, the practice must ensure that risks regarding the relevant procedure have been advised to the patient or substitute decision maker where necessary. Guideline statement: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audits. policy is consistent with the confidentiality and security of such information. When the retention period is complete, patient records and other data and information are destroyed appropriately. The policy will define: a) Levels of access for individual staff members b) The user's obligation to keep information confidential c) The process followed when confidentiality and/or security are violated d) Secure storage and retrieval of paper based and electronic patient records e) The protection of records against damage, fire, flood, theft, loss and electronic failure f) The arrangements for succession of patient records in the event of closure of the practice or death of the practitioner g) The criteria for selection and method of destruction of patient records Personnel members responsible for health record management must have suitable training and experience. Patient records must be readily available each time the patient visits the practice and therefore must be filed in such a way that they are easily identified.

criteria.description: Policies and procedures for the storage, retrieval, retention, destruction and confidentiality and safety of patient records are implemented.
criteria.guideline: Policies and procedures must include at least a) – g) in the standard intent above.

criteria.description: Designated individuals are responsible for the storage, maintenance and retrieval of patient files.
criteria.guideline: Only those designated individuals may have access to the patient records. There must be evidence of signed confidentiality agreements with each individual who has access to patient information.

criteria.description: The filing system allows for incorrectly filed records to be easily identified (e.g. through colour coding of the records)
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by observation.

criteria.description: Storage space for health records is sufficient and secure against unauthorised entry.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by observation.

criteria.description: Records that have been removed from the designated storage area for use are kept out of public view.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by observation. 3.3.1.6. CRITICAL The retention process provides the necessary confidentiality and security. Guideline statement: This will be confirmed by observation. examination and discharge/discontinuation of intervention, and other patient/client management activities. A standardised format and content of a patient's record will help promote the integration and continuity of care among the various providers of care to the patient. The practice determines the specific data and information recorded in the clinical record but it contains as a minimum for each consultation: a) The date of the consultation b) Who conducted the consultation c) Patient reason for consultation d) Results of the initial examination/assessment and evaluation e) Diagnosis f) Any medication prescribed for the patient (including name, strength, directions for use/dose frequency, number of repeats and date medication started/ceased/changed) g) Interventions/treatment and responses to interventions/treatment h) Changes in patient/client status relative to the interventions/treatment i) Re-examination and discharge/discontinuation of interventions. j) Any referral to other healthcare providers or health services k) Any special advice or other instructions l) Follow up instructions given to the patient m) For emergency cases relevant times are recorded, i.e. time patient seen, time drugs administered, etc. n) Patient allergies 3.4.1. In consultation with the patient, the practice provides a service that is consistent with best available evidence and in accordance with the patient’s identified needs and values.

standards.statement: Patient records contain the required information for the relevant condition.
standards.intent_tooltip: The clinical record of each patient, whether handwritten or electronic, clearly documents all aspects of the patient/client management, including the results of the initial examination/assessment and evaluation, diagnosis, prognosis/plan of care, intervention/treatment, response to interventions/treatment, changes in patient/client status relative to the interventions/treatment, re- 3.3.1.1. Policies and procedures for the storage, retrieval, retention, destruction and confidentiality and safety of patient records are implemented. Guideline statement: Policies and procedures must include at least a) – g) in the standard intent above. 3.3.1.2. Designated individuals are responsible for the storage, maintenance and retrieval of patient files. Guideline statement: Only those designated individuals may have access to the patient records. There must be evidence of signed confidentiality agreements with each individual who has access to patient information. 3.3.1.3. The filing system allows for incorrectly filed records to be easily identified (e.g. through colour coding of the records) Guideline statement: Compliance will be measured by observation. 3.3.1.4. Storage space for health records is sufficient and secure against unauthorised entry. Guideline statement: Compliance will be measured by observation. 3.3.1.5. Records that have been removed from the designated storage area for use are kept out of public view. Guideline statement: Compliance will be measured by observation. 3.3.1.6. CRITICAL The retention process provides the necessary confidentiality and security. Guideline statement: This will be confirmed by observation. examination and discharge/discontinuation of intervention, and other patient/client management activities. A standardised format and content of a patient's record will help promote the integration and continuity of care among the various providers of care to the patient. The practice determines the specific data and information recorded in the clinical record but it contains as a minimum for each consultation: a) The date of the consultation b) Who conducted the consultation c) Patient reason for consultation d) Results of the initial examination/assessment and evaluation e) Diagnosis f) Any medication prescribed for the patient (including name, strength, directions for use/dose frequency, number of repeats and date medication started/ceased/changed) g) Interventions/treatment and responses to interventions/treatment h) Changes in patient/client status relative to the interventions/treatment i) Re-examination and discharge/discontinuation of interventions. j) Any referral to other healthcare providers or health services k) Any special advice or other instructions l) Follow up instructions given to the patient m) For emergency cases relevant times are recorded, i.e. time patient seen, time drugs administered, etc. n) Patient allergies

criteria.description: Notes for each consultation contain as a minimum points a) – n) above.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.description: Handwritten notes are legible.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.description: Notes are recorded contemporaneously.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.description: All abbreviations are standardised according to recognised local and national standards.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.description: The patients’ records, including contact details, are up to date to ensure the transfer of the latest information between care providers.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.description: The patient is fully informed regarding the estimated costs of any treatment, investigation, procedure or referral prior to these costs being incurred and the discussion is documented.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit. Intent of 3.4.1 Consistency in the approach to diagnosis and management of care among those who are involved in the clinical care of an individual patient is an important aspect of continuity of care. Patients value consistency in the quality of treatment they receive from a practice and expect that treatment and advice given by different dietitians/therapist's within the practice will not be in conflict. Clinical practice guidelines provide important recommendations for clinical care and should be accessible at the point of care. Practices need to check that clinical practice guidelines are current. 3.4.2. The dietitian performs an initial assessment and evaluation to establish a diagnosis and prognosis/plan of care prior to intervention/treatment. Intent of 3.4.2 In order to evaluate the patient's needs and clinical requirements, a thorough examination should be done which includes a documented clinical history, review or request of necessary tests and investigations to assist with the treatment plan.

standards.statement: In consultation with the patient, the practice provides a service that is consistent with best available evidence and in accordance with the patient’s identified needs and values. 3.3.2.1. Notes for each consultation contain as a minimum points a) – n) above. Guideline statement: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit. 3.3.2.2. Handwritten notes are legible. Guideline statement: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit. 3.3.2.3. Notes are recorded contemporaneously. Guideline statement: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit. 3.3.2.4. All abbreviations are standardised according to recognised local and national standards. Guideline statement: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit. 3.3.2.5. The patients’ records, including contact details, are up to date to ensure the transfer of the latest information between care providers. Guideline statement: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit. 3.3.2.6. The patient is fully informed regarding the estimated costs of any treatment, investigation, procedure or referral prior to these costs being incurred and the discussion is documented. Guideline statement: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.
standards.intent_tooltip: Consistency in the approach to diagnosis and management of care among those who are involved in the clinical care of an individual patient is an important aspect of continuity of care. Patients value consistency in the quality of treatment they receive from a practice and expect that treatment and advice given by different dietitians/therapist's within the practice will not be in conflict. Clinical practice guidelines provide important recommendations for clinical care and should be accessible at the point of care. Practices need to check that clinical practice guidelines are current.

criteria.description: The clinical team uses current clinical guidelines relevant to clinical nutritional/dietetics services to assist in the assessment and management of patients.
criteria.guideline: Guidelines must be available at the point of care and should include high risk, high volume, high cost and rarely seen conditions. The practitioner must consider national guidelines where these are available. The emphasis in this criterion is on the availability and use of clinical guidelines and evidence of both must be provided for compliance.

criteria.description: The clinical team can describe how they ensure consistency of assessment, evaluation and management of patients.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured during personnel interviews and the patient record audit.

criteria.description: The clinical team can demonstrate how they communicate about clinical issues and support systems in the practice.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured during personnel interviews and the patient record audit.

standards.statement: The dietitian performs an initial assessment and evaluation to establish a diagnosis and prognosis/plan of care prior to intervention/treatment.
standards.intent_tooltip: In order to evaluate the patient's needs and clinical requirements, a thorough examination should be done which includes a documented clinical history, review or request of necessary tests and investigations to assist with the treatment plan.

criteria.description: The dietitians assessment is documented, dated, and appropriately authenticated by the dietitian who performed it.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by a review of patient records.

criteria.description: The nutritional assessment identifies the dietary/nutritional needs of the patient/client.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by a review of patient records.

criteria.description: The dietitians examination incorporates appropriate history taking, systems review and tests and measures to facilitate outcome measurement.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by reviewing patient records.

criteria.description: The assessment produces data which is sufficient to allow evaluation, diagnosis, prognosis/plan of care, and determination of interventions/treatment.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by reviewing patient records.

criteria.description: The nutritional assessment may result in recommendations for additional services to meet the needs of the patient/client.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by reviewing patient records.

criteria.description: The assessment provides written evidence of the reasons why no further management has been given to the patient/client and, when appropriate, to the carer. 3.4.3. The dietitian determines the interventions/treatments and manages the needs of the patient/client based on the examination, evaluation, diagnosis, prognosis/plan, anticipated goals and expected outcomes of the planned interventions/treatments for identified impairments, activity limitations and participation restrictions and/or for prevention, health promotion, fitness, and wellness. Intent of 3.4.3 The dietitians' s treatment plan should take into account the findings of all investigations performed, and should consult with the multi-disciplinary team in order to achieve the best outcome for the patient. Communication between the clinical care team is of utmost importance in achieving the anticipated goals for each patient.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by reviewing patient records.

standards.statement: The dietitian determines the interventions/treatments and manages the needs of the patient/client based on the examination, evaluation, diagnosis, prognosis/plan, anticipated goals and expected outcomes of the planned interventions/treatments for identified impairments, activity limitations and participation restrictions and/or for prevention, health promotion, fitness, and wellness.
standards.intent_tooltip: The dietitians' s treatment plan should take into account the findings of all investigations performed, and should consult with the multi-disciplinary team in order to achieve the best outcome for the patient. Communication between the clinical care team is of utmost importance in achieving the anticipated goals for each patient.

criteria.description: The dietitian collaboratively involves the patient/client and others as appropriate in the planning, implementation, and assessment of the intervention/treatment.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by reviewing patient records.

criteria.description: The intervention/treatment is based on the initial assessment, evaluation, diagnosis, and prognosis/plan and informed by current evidence.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by reviewing patient records in conjunction with the available and implemented clinical guidelines.

criteria.description: The intervention/treatment identifies anticipated goals and expected outcomes describes the proposed intervention/treatment, including frequency and duration.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by reviewing patient records.

criteria.description: The intervention/treatment is at a level that is consistent with current clinical guidelines for a dietetics practice.
criteria.guideline: The practice regularly reviews it’s standard treatment protocols and aligns it to current international best practice and clinical guidelines.

criteria.description: The intervention/treatment is inter-professional when necessary to meet the needs of the patient/client.
criteria.guideline: The practice regularly communicates with the multi-disciplinary team regarding details of the patient’s clinical management and progression.

criteria.description: The intervention/treatment includes documentation that is dated and appropriately authenticated by the dietitian who established the plan.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by reviewing patient records. 3.4.3.7. CRITICAL Interventions/treatments provided by support personnel is directed and supervised by the principal dietitian. Guideline statement: Compliance will be verified by reviewing patient records and practical observation.

criteria.description: Interventions/treatments provided by support personnel are commensurate with the qualifications and the legal limitations of such personnel.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by reviewing patient records, personnel files and clinical practice protocols.

criteria.description: Interventions/treatments provided by support personnel may only be altered by the dietitian in accordance with changes in response or status.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by reviewing patient records, personnel files and clinical practice protocols.

criteria.description: Interventions/treatments provided by support personnel includes documentation of the intervention/treatment that is consistent with established guidelines and that is dated appropriately and authenticated by the dietitian. 3.4.4. The dietitian re-assesses the patient/client as necessary during an episode to evaluate progress or change in patient/client status and modifies the plan accordingly or discontinues dietetics services. Intent of 3.4.4 The dietitian should conduct a regular review of the patient's treatment plan and should reassess the patient at regular intervals to determine if the desired outcomes are being achieved with the current recommended treatment plan. The details of the such reviewal and reassessment should be documented timeously. 3.5.1. The dietitian discharges the patient/client from the services when the anticipated goals or expected outcomes for the patient/client have been achieved or the patient/client status reveals findings outside the scope of knowledge, experience, and/or expertise of the dietitian. Intent of 3.5.1 The dietitian must discharge the patient when the needs of the patient has been met, or the therapist can longer assist with the progression of the patient's anticipated care.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by reviewing patient records in conjunction with the available and implemented clinical guidelines, as well as personnel files and clinical practice protocols.

standards.statement: The dietitian re-assesses the patient/client as necessary during an episode to evaluate progress or change in patient/client status and modifies the plan accordingly or discontinues dietetics services.
standards.intent_tooltip: The dietitian should conduct a regular review of the patient's treatment plan and should reassess the patient at regular intervals to determine if the desired outcomes are being achieved with the current recommended treatment plan. The details of the such reviewal and reassessment should be documented timeously.

criteria.description: The re-assessment is documented, dated, and appropriately authenticated by the dietitian who performs it.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by reviewing patient records.

criteria.description: The re-assessment includes modifications to the intervention/treatment or referral to other practitioner/professionals.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by reviewing patient records.

criteria.description: The dietitian obtains the patients consent for any modifications to the treatment where necessary.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by reviewing patient records.

standards.statement: The dietitian discharges the patient/client from the services when the anticipated goals or expected outcomes for the patient/client have been achieved or the patient/client status reveals findings outside the scope of knowledge, experience, and/or expertise of the dietitian.
standards.intent_tooltip: The dietitian must discharge the patient when the needs of the patient has been met, or the therapist can longer assist with the progression of the patient's anticipated care.

criteria.description: The dietitian discontinues intervention/treatment when the patient/client is unable to continue to progress toward goals or when the dietitian determines that the patient/client will no longer benefit from the dietetics service.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by reviewing patient records.

criteria.description: The dietitian informs the patient that the patient/client has a right to discontinue treatment/intervention at any time.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by reviewing patient records.

criteria.description: Where the assessment, re-assessment, or any change in status reveals findings outside the scope of knowledge, experience, and/or expertise of the dietitian, the patient/client shall so be informed and referred to the appropriate practitioner/professional.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by reviewing patient records. 3.5.2. There is a process for appropriate referral of patients for specialised consultation/investigations at other healthcare facilities. Intent of 3.5.2 In some cases, dietitians refer patients for a secondary consultation to confirm an opinion, or request more extensive evaluations than may be available to the dietitian or to have patients receive specialised treatment that the practice may be unable to provide. The practice must clearly describe the referral process. Practices may wish to consider the use of a standard referral form which includes a tear off slip for the receiving therapist's response following the consultation. The referral should contain the following information: a) at least three approved patient identifiers b) relevant history, examination findings and current management c) known allergies, adverse drug reactions and current medication d) the name of the specialist referred to e) stated purpose of the referral f) request for feedback following the consultation with the specialist

standards.statement: There is a process for appropriate referral of patients for specialised consultation/investigations at other healthcare facilities.
standards.intent_tooltip: In some cases, dietitians refer patients for a secondary consultation to confirm an opinion, or request more extensive evaluations than may be available to the dietitian or to have patients receive specialised treatment that the practice may be unable to provide. The practice must clearly describe the referral process. Practices may wish to consider the use of a standard referral form which includes a tear off slip for the receiving therapist's response following the consultation. The referral should contain the following information: a) at least three approved patient identifiers b) relevant history, examination findings and current management c) known allergies, adverse drug reactions and current medication d) the name of the specialist referred to e) stated purpose of the referral f) request for feedback following the consultation with the specialist

criteria.description: There is a documented process to refer patients.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.description: The lines of communication between the practice, referral centres and community services are clearly defined.
criteria.guideline: Practice personnel must clearly describe the referral process, especially where patients are sent to another healthcare organisation for specialist consultation or special investigations and then return to the referring practice for further investigation. Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.description: The dietitian informs the patient if he/she has any financial interest in the referral service.
criteria.guideline: Surveyors must pay particular attention to services that are offered in the same facility by different practitioners for example, where a general practitioner refers a patient to a radiology or laboratory practice in the same facility.

criteria.description: Referrals are to specific individuals and/or agencies in the patient's home community wherever possible.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.description: Patients and as appropriate their families are given follow-up instructions which are provided in an understandable form and manner.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.description: A copy of the referral note is available in the patient record.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.description: The referral is made on appropriate stationery or electronically.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.description: The referral contains as a minimum a) – f) in the standard intent above.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.description: The practice keeps a record of replies received following the referral of patients. 3.6.1. The practice has an effective clinical handover system that ensures safe and continuing healthcare delivery for patients. Intent of 3.6.1 Clinical handover needs to occur whenever there is an interface of care by different providers. Examples of clinical handover include: • A dietitian covering for a fellow therapist who is on leave or is unexpectedly absent • A dietitian covering for a part time colleague • A dietitian handing over care to another health professional such as a practice nurse, physiotherapist, podiatrist, psychologist or general practitioner. • A dietitian referring a patient to a service outside the practice • A shared care arrangement (e.g. team care of a patient with mental health problems) After hours care providers are at a disadvantage with regards to specific groups of patients requiring special attention such as the terminally ill. To ensure optimum care provision and a consistent approach from all healthcare providers, it is to the advantage of both patients and therapists if relevant information is made available to the health professionals providing after hours care. The sharing of information relating to infection control (e.g. HIV, TB, Hep B, MRSA) will enable those providing care outside the practice (after hours, secondary care, etc.) to offer the patient and persons coming into contact with the affected patient appropriate care and precautionary measures. To ensure the transfer of this information, the patient is provided with a letter to present to health professionals providing care outside the practice.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence must be provided of the system that is used in the practice to record and act upon replies received from referral practitioners.

criteria.description: There are written guidelines for the referral of emergency patients where applicable.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence is required.

criteria.description: Follow-up care based on the findings of investigations/consultations performed outside the practice is noted in the patient record.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

standards.statement: The practice has an effective clinical handover system that ensures safe and continuing healthcare delivery for patients.
standards.intent_tooltip: Clinical handover needs to occur whenever there is an interface of care by different providers. Examples of clinical handover include: • A dietitian covering for a fellow therapist who is on leave or is unexpectedly absent • A dietitian covering for a part time colleague • A dietitian handing over care to another health professional such as a practice nurse, physiotherapist, podiatrist, psychologist or general practitioner. • A dietitian referring a patient to a service outside the practice • A shared care arrangement (e.g. team care of a patient with mental health problems) After hours care providers are at a disadvantage with regards to specific groups of patients requiring special attention such as the terminally ill. To ensure optimum care provision and a consistent approach from all healthcare providers, it is to the advantage of both patients and therapists if relevant information is made available to the health professionals providing after hours care. The sharing of information relating to infection control (e.g. HIV, TB, Hep B, MRSA) will enable those providing care outside the practice (after hours, secondary care, etc.) to offer the patient and persons coming into contact with the affected patient appropriate care and precautionary measures. To ensure the transfer of this information, the patient is provided with a letter to present to health professionals providing care outside the practice.

criteria.description: There is implementation of a documented policy on clinical handover to ensure that standard processes are followed.
criteria.guideline: The complexity and comprehensiveness of the content of handover information required to achieve continuity of care will differ for different types of handover situations. The details to be included in each situation should be agreed upon by all stakeholders and formalised in a practice to ensure a standardised approach at all times. The transfer of information must be documented, preferably using standardised forms (electronic or paper-based) for the various handovers. Compliance will be verified during patient record audit.

criteria.description: Patients share in decision making regarding the handing over of clinical information. 3.6.2. The practice ensures safe and reasonable arrangements for clinical care for patients outside of normal opening hours. Intent of 3.6.2 The dietetics service outside normal opening hours needs to be provided by recognised dietitians. When the practice's therapist's themselves cannot safely or reasonably deliver care outside normal opening hours, the practice can clearly document the alternative system of care that is available for their patients. The practice is responsible for ensuring the alternative care provider offers care to an acceptable standard. 3.7.1. The practice provides emergency treatment and care. Intent of 3.7.1 Emergency treatment of patients may be unpredictable, particularly in practices that do not form part of a 24-hour service. The practice must be prepared to provide primary emergency treatment as required to stabilise a patient while waiting for further assistance from emergency medical services.
criteria.guideline: Patients also have a responsibility for their own health and must share in the decision of who will continue with their care. Compliance will be verified by documentation that the patient has been informed and shared in the decision of handing over.

criteria.description: Handover of clinical information is recorded in the patients' health records.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.description: Information relating to those patients requiring special attention, such as the chronically ill or those with infections which require specific infection control measures, is provided in a patient held letter, a copy of which is kept in the patient record.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

standards.statement: The practice ensures safe and reasonable arrangements for clinical care for patients outside of normal opening hours.
standards.intent_tooltip: The dietetics service outside normal opening hours needs to be provided by recognised dietitians. When the practice's therapist's themselves cannot safely or reasonably deliver care outside normal opening hours, the practice can clearly document the alternative system of care that is available for their patients. The practice is responsible for ensuring the alternative care provider offers care to an acceptable standard.

criteria.description: There is evidence of one (or a combination) of arrangements for patients to access care outside of normal opening hours.
criteria.guideline: Regardless of the arrangements used to provide care outside normal opening hours, documented evidence of the system the practice uses to provide such care needs to be available.

criteria.description: The credentials of alternative care providers are verified.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence must be provided.

criteria.description: Patient health records contain reports or notes of dietetics service occurring outside normal opening hours by or on behalf of the practice.
criteria.guideline: This criterion will not be applicable to consultations that occur at public health facilities. Evidence must be provided on how and when the practice receives notes of investigations occurring through all other after-hours arrangements. Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

standards.statement: The practice provides emergency treatment and care.
standards.intent_tooltip: Emergency treatment of patients may be unpredictable, particularly in practices that do not form part of a 24-hour service. The practice must be prepared to provide primary emergency treatment as required to stabilise a patient while waiting for further assistance from emergency medical services.

criteria.description: Written guidelines are available and followed relating to the provision of primary emergency services.
criteria.guideline: As a minimum, the Basic Life Support algorithm must be available and followed. Documented evidence of training must be provided. 3.7.2. The practice provides resuscitation in accordance with practice policy. Intent of 3.7.2 Resuscitation policies and equipment should be standardised throughout the practice. Standardised resuscitation processes require: a) The level at which resuscitation is provided and by whom b) Coordination among those who provide and maintain the equipment c) Availability of required equipment d) Initial and ongoing training of personnel in the use of equipment and execution of procedures e) Maintenance and monitoring of equipment f) Current, evidence-based guidelines for resuscitation Deficiencies in the system regarding equipment, its use and the knowledge and skills required by those who carry out resuscitation should be identified, documented and acted upon. Each practice should identify those members of personnel to be trained in emergency life support and the level of training (basic or advanced) appropriate to their role in the practice. The person(s) providing the training must be currently registered and/or accredited with a recognised body as a resuscitation trainer. Training in many instances can be outsourced.

criteria.description: Guidelines for paediatric emergency triage, assessment and treatment (ETAT) are available and followed.
criteria.guideline: The practice’s policy on triage must make distinction between triage undertaken by members of the clinical team and triage undertaken by personnel with non-clinical roles. Appropriate training must be provided to assist both administrative personnel and members of the clinical team to identify patients in need or urgent care. Documented evidence of training must be provided.
